coredata.dc:title: Blue TiO2 polymorph: An efficient material for dye-sensitized solar cells fabricated using a low-temperature sintering process
coredata.prism:publicationName: Progress in Natural Science: Materials International
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Chinese Materials Research Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Materials Research Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Huy Hao
Gyawali, Gobinda
Kim, Tae Ho
Bin Humam, Sami
Lee, Soo Wohn
coredata.dc:description: Recently, surface-disordered or non-stoichiometric TiO2, such as blue TiO2 (B-TiO2), has received much attention owing to its unique properties, such as structural disorder near the surface and the existence of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies. Therefore, surface-disordered TiO2 has been applied for microwave absorption, photocatalysis, in photoelectrochemical sensors and rechargeable lithium-ion batteries. In this work B-TiO2, a polymorph consisting of nanoparticles, nanotubes and nanosheets, was successfully synthesized and employed as a semiconductor layer in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) fabricated using a low-temperature heat treatment process (120 °C). Based on the analyses of the experimental results regarding the structure and those from the characterization of B-TiO2, and its application to DSSCs, it has been found that the B-TiO2 material has an effect on electron-hole pair separation. The conversion efficiency of the B-TiO2 DSSC (BTiO2-DSSC) was 6.18%, whereas that of the TiO2-P25 DSSC (P25-DSSC) was 3.61%, and that of the TiO2 polymorph DSSC (PTiO2-DSSC) which is the precursor of B-TiO2 was 4.51%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-disordered TiO2
Blue TiO2
Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC)
Oxygen vacancy
Efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 pastes for DSSCs
Fabrication of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2.
HRTEM images of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2.
Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of the B-TiO2.

XPS spectra of samples; a) Survey spectra of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2, b) Ti 2p state of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2, c) O 1s, state of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2, d) deconvolution of Ti 2p B-TiO2, e) deconvolution of O 1s-P25, f) deconvolution of O 1s-P-TiO2, and g) deconvolution of O 1s B-TiO2.
The photocurrent-voltage (I-V) curve of the P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC.
Photoluminescence spectra of the P25, P-TiO2, and the B-TiO2.
Incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra of the P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC.
Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) with fitting equivalent circuit data of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC.
The TiO2 photo-anode films of fabricated DSSCs.
Photovoltaic parameters of the P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC.
Series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rpt), and electron transfer resistance (Rct) of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC.

head.ce:title: Blue TiO2 polymorph: An efficient material for dye-sensitized solar cells fabricated using a low-temperature sintering process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental and Bio-chemical Engineering, Sun Moon University, Asan, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental and Bio-chemical Engineering, Sun Moon University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Basic Engineering, Sun Moon University, Asan, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Basic Engineering, Sun Moon University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Mechanics and ICT Convergence Engineering, Sun Moon University, Asan, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Mechanics and ICT Convergence Engineering, Sun Moon University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, surface-disordered or non-stoichiometric TiO2, such as blue TiO2 (B-TiO2), has received much attention owing to its unique properties, such as structural disorder near the surface and the existence of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies. Therefore, surface-disordered TiO2 has been applied for microwave absorption, photocatalysis, in photoelectrochemical sensors and rechargeable lithium-ion batteries. In this work B-TiO2, a polymorph consisting of nanoparticles, nanotubes and nanosheets, was successfully synthesized and employed as a semiconductor layer in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) fabricated using a low-temperature heat treatment process (120 °C). Based on the analyses of the experimental results regarding the structure and those from the characterization of B-TiO2, and its application to DSSCs, it has been found that the B-TiO2 material has an effect on electron-hole pair separation. The conversion efficiency of the B-TiO2 DSSC (BTiO2-DSSC) was 6.18%, whereas that of the TiO2-P25 DSSC (P25-DSSC) was 3.61%, and that of the TiO2 polymorph DSSC (PTiO2-DSSC) which is the precursor of B-TiO2 was 4.51%.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are considered to be the potential next generation of photovoltaic devices owing to some advantages for industrial applications such as low-cost technology and simple fabrication procedures [1–5]. Typically, a DSSC consists of a semiconductor film, a photosensitive dye, an electrolyte and a Pt counter electrode. For DSSCs, each part may have a specific effect on their costs and energy conversion efficiencies. Thus, much effort has been focused on the modification of each component to improve the performances of DSSCs. For example, quasi-solid, solid-liquid, and solid electrolytes have been studied as replacements for liquid electrolytes to address the leaking problems of liquid electrolytes and extend the active lifetimes of DSSCs [6–10]. Recently, natural dyes have been used instead of metal complex dyes to reduce the manufacturing costs. Among the modifications performed to enhance the efficiencies of DSSCs, great efforts have been devoted to those of the host TiO2, because this semiconductor material, which acts as a framework for DSSCs, is one of the most important components.

Surface-disordered TiO2, which consists of a core-shell structure with a lattice core and an amorphous shell, has emerged recently as a potential candidate in the field of advanced materials. The existence of superficial defects such as Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies in non-stoichiometric TiO2 is exciting because they may alter the band gap energy, leading to the expansion of the optical absorption range for visible light [11–14]. Surface-disordered TiO2 has been applied in photocatalysts [12,15–17], photoelectrochemical sensors [18], catalysts [19], rechargeable lithium-ion batteries [20], and supercapacitors [21], in field emission [22] and microwave absorption [23,24]. For DSSC applications, high-temperature sintering is normally required to remove the binder from the TiO2 paste to achieve good electric contact at the interface between the TiO2 film and the substrate, as well as to enhance the mechanical strength of the semiconductor layer [25–27]. However, the disorder in the layers of the TiO2 surface may become anatase during the sintering of the TiO2 film at 450 °C in the DSSC fabrication process. Hence, this process limits the application of non-stoichiometric blue TiO2 to DSSCs. Although there are several reports concerning the use of non-stoichiometric TiO2 in DSSCs, a detailed study on the effects of the Ti3+ state and oxygen vacancies on the power conversion efficiencies of DSSCs is still lacking.

To tackle the above-mentioned issues, DSSCs were fabricated with disordered blue TiO2 (B-TiO2) using a low-temperature heat treatment process (120 °C). We also demonstrated that B-TiO2 plays a role in the effective separation of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the performances of DSSCs.

Commercial Degussa TiO2-P25 (P25) was employed as pristine TiO2. NaOH (98% purity), metallic Li foil, and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and HCl (35.5%) was purchased from Duksan Chemicals. All of these chemicals were used without further purification.

The preparation method was based on authors’ previous work [3]. First, 1 g of P25 was dissolved in 50 mL of 10 M NaOH at room temperature. The mixture was then stirred by a magnetic stirrer for 30 min at room temperature. Afterwards, the suspension was transferred to a Teflon vessel reactor for the microwave-assisted hydrothermal process, which was conducted at 100 °C, 195 W, and a rotation speed of 300 rpm for 4 h. Following this step, the obtained solid was washed with 0.5 M HCl and distilled water. Finally, P-TiO2 was dried in a freeze dryer (EYELA, FDU-2100) for 24 h at − 80 °C.

The synthesis followed the method reported by Zhang et al. [28]. First, 1.0 g of the above P-TiO2 and 0.7 g of Li foil were placed in a reactor. This process was conducted in an Ar-filled glove box with O2 and H2O contents both below 10 ppm. Then, the reactor was connected to a vacuum pump for 30 min to ensure complete removal of oxygen and moisture from the sample. Then, a nitrogen-filled balloon was attached to this reactor in order to maintain an inert atmosphere during the reaction. In the next step, 100 mL of ethylenediamine was added to the reaction system, which was then stirred for one week. After the reaction was complete, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7 by adding 5 N HCl. The mixture was washed with distilled water, and B-TiO2 was collected and dried at 50 °C for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 pastes for DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The pastes were prepared using the ball-milling method. In a typical procedure, a mixture containing 1 g of P25 (or P-TiO2 or B-TiO2) and 7.5 mL of ethanol was added to a plastic bottle containing Al2O3 balls of various sizes. The mixture was then milled at 200 rpm, and the TiO2 paste (P25, P-TiO2, or B-TiO2 paste) was collected after milling for 20 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, three different double-layer DSSCs were fabricated using the doctor blade method. The compositions of the top layers and underlayers in the three different TiO2 photoanode films are summarized in Table 1.

First, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glasses 1.5 cm × 2 cm in size were cleaned with ethanol and dried at 80 °C for 15 min. These pieces of glass were then immersed in an aqueous 90 mM TiCl4 solution at 70 °C for 30 min, dried, and heat-treated in an oven at 120 °C for 2 h. Next, a layer of P25 paste was coated onto the substrates using a spin coater (Spin coater ACE-200) at 4500 rpm for 15 s. Then, this first layer was covered completely by a second layer containing only P25 (P25-DSSC), P-TiO2 (PTiO2-DSSC), or B-TiO2 (BTiO2-DSSC) through the same spin coating method. Following this step, the photoanodes were heated again at 120 °C for 5 h, treated again with the TiCl4 solution at 70 °C for 30 min and dried in a furnace at 120 °C for 5 h before being placed into an N719 dye solution for 24 h.
Finally, the working electrode was connected to a Pt/glass counter electrode. The electrolyte was then added to produce the DSSC.

The structural analysis of P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku, RINT-2200 with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) patterns between 5° and 70°. The microstructures of all samples were observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and the elements in the samples were detected using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (FEI Titan™ 80–300). To investigate the chemical properties, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an XR5 Gun-500 µm X-ray monochromator from Thermo Scientific. A photoluminescence spectrometer (Shamrock 303i, Andor Technology Ltd., UK) at a 325 nm excitation wavelength was used to study the electron-hole recombination in the materials. The power conversion efficiencies of the DSSCs were measured under a simulated solar light source (Peccell PEC-L01 portable solar simulator, W) (Am 1.5 G). The incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) and the electrochemical impedance spectra were measured using an IVIUMSTAT electrochemical interface analyzer (the Netherlands).

The XRD patterns of P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 are shown in Fig. 1a. The anatase phase of the P25 precursor can be seen from the XRD peaks at 25.36°, 37.06°, 37.90°, 38.58°, 48.12°, 54.06°, 55.18°, 62.80°, and 68.96° (JCPDS No. 21-1272), and the rutile phase can be seen from the peaks at 27.50°, 36.14°, 41.28°, 44.04°, and 56.74° (JCPDS No. 46-1238). The anatase and rutile phase peaks were also found in the pattern of P-TiO2 at 48.12° (anatase), 27.50°, 36.14°, and 41.28° (rutile). Moreover, titanate phase with low crystallinity existed in P-TiO2, as indicated by the peaks at 10.1°, 24.4°, and 54.3°. The appearance of the titanate phase is expected since TiO2 nanotubes, nanosheets, or nanoribbons may form in P-TiO2. Fig. 1b reveals that all titanate peaks at 10.1°, 24.4°, and 54.3° and the rutile peaks at 36.14° and 41.28° were absent for the B-TiO2 sample, and only two peaks at 27.24° and 47.88° for the rutile and anatase phases, respectively, remained. It is worth noting that the absence of some rutile and titanate peaks is associated with the formation of the blue TiO2 material. According to Zhang et al. [28] and Finnegan et al. [29], while the anatase phase is stabilized in a strong base medium, the rutile phase tends to receive damage and transform into an amorphous structure. Hence, the absence of the rutile phase could be explained by the presence of ethylenediamine, which acts as a superbase. This observation is similar to a published result [28].

Fig. 2 shows HRTEM images of the P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 samples. It is clear that P25 has nanoparticle morphology while the P-TiO2 and B-TiO2 samples are composed of TiO2 nanoparticles, nanotubes and nanosheets. The co-existence of different morphologies in the synthesized P-TiO2 and B-TiO2 is expected to enhance the scattering of incident solar radiation within the cell and assist dye molecules for higher absorptivity in the TiO2 film layer.

EDS was employed to determine the residual lithium in the B-TiO2 sample ( Fig. 3). However, the peak corresponding to Li did not appear. This confirmed that lithium was completely removed during the washing process and that there was no residual lithium in the sample. Moreover, it is worth noting that the appearance of a Cu peak was due to the Cu substrate used in the TiO2 coating during the EDS measurement.

To study the chemical and electronic states of the elements in the TiO2 samples, XPS measurements were carried out. Fig. 4a–g give information about the binding energies and electronic environments of the constituent elements of P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 [30]. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 4a show that the three samples were composed of Ti and O; the appearance of the C 1 s peak at 286 eV was due to the carbon that was used as a substrate for the XPS measurements. The Ti 2p states in P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2 are shown in Fig. 4b. As can be seen, each sample has two Ti 2p peaks at approximately 460 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2) and 465 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2). The deconvoluted Ti 2p spectrum of blue TiO2 (Fig. 4d) shows a less intense peak at 458.6 eV and a broad peak at 463 eV, which were assigned to Ti3+ 2p. In the case of the P25 and P-TiO2 samples, no new de-convoluted peaks appeared. The presence of Ti3+ 2p in B-TiO2 could be explained through the change of the rutile and titanate phases into amorphous. According to Tian et al. [31], this amorphous phase is composed of Ti2O3. Hence, the appearance of the blue color of the TiO2 product is due to the formation of a Ti2O3 phase during the reaction process.

Fig. 4c shows the XPS peaks contributed by the O 1s binding energy states of P25, P-TiO2, and B-TiO2, which are at 531.2, 531.1, and 531.3 eV, respectively. These XPS spectra were deconvoluted and are shown in Fig. 4e–g. As can be seen in Fig. 4e and f, the O2− species of P25 and P-TiO2 gave two deconvoluted peaks related to lattice oxygen in TiO2 (OL) and chemisorbed oxygen (OC) [32]. The OC/OL concentration ratios calculated from the peak areas were 84.7% for P25 and 104% for P-TiO2. The higher OC/OL concentration ratio of P-TiO2 compared to that of P25 can be explained through the larger particle size of P-TiO2, which was revealed in HRTEM images. For B-TiO2, one new peak corresponding to oxygen vacancy states was found at 458.6 eV (Fig. 4g); however, this oxygen vacancy peak was not present in the P25 and P-TiO2 spectra.

The OC/OL concentration ratio of P-TiO2 is 104%, while that of B-TiO2 was found to be 72.5%. It is worth mentioning that the lower OC/OL concentration ratio of B-TiO2 is associated with the formation of OV and Ti3+. This result suggests that during the formation of blue TiO2, Ti4+ receives electrons from OC to produce Ti3+ and OV. The formation of Ti3+ and OV can be explained through the following chemical reaction:
Ti4+ + e (OL) → Ti3+ (Ti2O3) + OV

Table 2 and Fig. 5 show the experimentally determined efficiency, short circuit current density (Jsc), open circuit voltage (Voc), and fill factor (FF) of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC. According to the above data, PTiO2-DSSC and BTiO2-DSSC showed better Jsc values than that of P25-DSSC. While larger particles have stronger light scattering, smaller particles possess higher surface areas and can adsorb a higher amount of photosensitive molecules. Therefore, a combination of large and small particles in the semiconductor film of PTiO2-DSSC and BTiO2-DSSC enhances both sunlight capture and dye adsorption. As a result, the power conversion efficiencies of PTiO2-DSSC and BTiO2-DSSC are higher than that of P25-DSSC.

The current density is affected by the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs in the TiO2 film layer. Thus, the Jsc data revealed that B-TiO2 plays a crucial role of reducing the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs. The effect of the B-TiO2 material on Jsc may be due to the disorder in Ti2O3. According to Zhang et al. [28] and Chen et al. [33], B-TiO2 consists of a crystalline TiO2 core and a thin layer of disordered shell. In the B-TiO2 film, the disordered shell may act as a thin membrane to prevent the recombination of photoelectrons and holes. Therefore, the number of photoelectrons migrating to the anode of BTiO2-DSSC is higher than that to the anode of PTiO2-DSSC, improving the photocurrent density.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra in Fig. 6 were measured to examine the electron-hole recombination in P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC [34]. The experimental results showed that the PL intensity of BTiO2-DSSC was lower than those of PTiO2-DSSC and P25-DSSC. This suggests that the recombination rate of the photo-generated charge carriers of BTiO2-DSSC is lower than those of the other DSSCs. Mercado et al. [35] reported that the oxygen vacancies could trap holes; thus, the positive carrier traps caused these carriers to be less mobile than usual, leading to an improvement in electron mobility and reducing charge recombination. As a result, the PL intensity of BTiO2-DSSC is lower than those of PTiO2-DSSC and P25-DSSC.

The IPCE ( Fig. 7) was measured to examine the conversion of incident light into electrical energy at a given wavelength. Below is the equation for calculating the IPCE values [36]:
IPCE
%
=
1240
eV
nm
x
J
I × λ
x 100 %
where I is the intensity of incident monochromatic light (mW cm−2) and J (mA cm−2) is the short circuit current density with monochromatic irradiation at a wavelength λ (nm). The IPCE values are proportional to the light harvesting efficiency and the photocurrent of the DSSCs. As mentioned above, larger particles have stronger light scattering properties compared to smaller ones. Therefore, the IPCEs of BTiO2-DSSC and PTiO2-DSSC, which contain large particles such as nanotubes and nanosheets, are higher than that of P25-DSSC, which consists of smaller-sized nanoparticles. Moreover, the existence of oxygen vacancies promotes the migration of more photoelectrons to the anode than usual, as stated before; therefore, the IPCE of BTiO2-DSSC was higher than that of PTiO2-DSSC.

Fig. 8 shows the fitting equivalent circuit electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of P25-DSSC, PTiO2-DSSC, and BTiO2-DSSC. EIS is conducted to examine the charge transfers and transport processes in DSSCs [37]. Typically, there are three key resistance values that can be quantified from EIS spectra. The Rs value is the sheet resistance of the FTO substrate. The resistance Rpt is related to the charge transfer resistance at the interface between the electrolyte and Pt electrode. Finally, the Rct value represents the electron transfer at the interface between the electrolyte, dye, and TiO2. These resistances are listed in Table 3. As can be seen in Table 3, the values of Rs and Rpt are similar for these three DSSCs. These results may have been due to the application of the same type of FTO glass and electrolyte (I−/I3 −). However, the Rct values of the three samples were different: 14.5 Ω for P25-DSSC, 12.3 Ω for PTiO2-DSSC and 9.7 Ω for BTiO2-DSSC. The lower Rct value for BTiO2-DSSC suggests that the resistance against the transfer of photoelectrons through the TiO2 film is lower, leading to higher efficiency for BTiO2-DSSC compared to that of P25-DSSC and PTiO2-DSSC.

In this work, B-TiO2 has been successfully applied to a DSSC via a low-temperature (120 °C) heat treatment process. The enhancement in the power conversion efficiency of BTiO2-DSSC has been studied with reference to the electron-hole recombination effect. The experimental findings from XPS, PL, and EIS analyses reveal the effects of the disordered shell, which suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhance electron migration to the anode. As a result, the efficiency of BTiO2-DSSC is significantly higher than those of P25-DSSC and PTiO2-DSSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Global Research Laboratory Program of the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (MEST) of Korea (Grant number: 2010-00339).
